perturbation.original_text: This Agreement may be terminated at all times with a 30 daysʼ prior written notice provided, however, that the confidentiality obligations herein shall terminate ____________ years following the date of termination of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement may be terminated at all times with a 5 days’ prior written notice provided, however, that the confidentiality obligations herein shall terminate ____________ years following the date of termination of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the termination notice period to 5 days creates a conflict with typical contract law standards, which often require a reasonable notice period that is longer than 5 days.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Reasonable Notice Period for Contract Termination
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 2-309 requires reasonable notification before termination of a contract when the agreement is indefinite in duration, implying that 5 days' notice might be deemed unreasonable depending on the context and industry standards.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-309. Absence of Specific Time Provisions; Notice of Termination. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-309. Absence of Specific Time Provisions; Notice of Termination. (1) The time for shipment or delivery or any other action under a contract if not provided in this Article or agreed upon shall be a reasonable time. (2) Where the contract provides for successive performances but is indefinite in duration it is valid for a reasonable time but unless otherwise agreed may be terminated at any time by either party. (3) Termination of a contract by one party except on the happening of an agreed event requires that reasonable notification be received by the other party and an agreement dispensing with notification is invalid if its operation would be unconscionable. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the termination notice period from 30 days to 5 days, creating a potential contradiction with UCC § 2-309. The Law Explanation states that UCC § 2-309 requires reasonable notification before termination of a contract. Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifically § 2-309 (3), states that termination requires "reasonable notification be received by the other party." The Perturbation Explanation notes that 5 days might be considered unreasonable. Therefore, the change introduces a significant risk of non-compliance, as 5 days may not be deemed 'reasonable notification' under the UCC, establishing a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed and enforced in accordance with the laws of the State/Territory ____________________________________________ as they apply to contracts entered into and wholly to be performed in the State/Territory of _______________________________________. The Federal and State courts within the State/Territory of _________________________________________ shall have exclusive jurisdiction to adjudicate any dispute arising out of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed and enforced in accordance with the laws of the State/Territory ____________________________________________ as they apply to contracts entered into and wholly to be performed in the State/Territory of _______________________________________. The courts within the State/Territory of _________________________________________ shall have non-exclusive jurisdiction to adjudicate any dispute arising out of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'exclusive jurisdiction' to 'non-exclusive jurisdiction' could conflict with standard contract law principles where parties agree to a specific venue for dispute resolution.  If that venue is not followed, it could lead to dismissal due to improper venue.
perturbation.law_explanation: While 28 U.S. Code § 1404 permits a court to transfer a case to another district, this statute assumes an initial proper venue. If the agreement specifies an exclusive jurisdiction that is ignored in favor of a non-exclusive venue, it violates the contract terms. A judge may view an exclusive jurisdiction clause as an agreement that venue is proper in only one jurisdiction. While 1404 allows for transfers for convenience and justice, that power is diminished if the original venue was part of a legal agreement.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 28 U.S. Code § 1404 - Change of venue | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 28 U.S. Code § 1404 - Change of venue U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) For the convenience of parties and witnesses, in the interest of justice, a district court may transfer any civil action to any other district or division where it might have been brought or to any district or division to which all parties have consented. (b) Upon motion, consent or stipulation of all parties, any action, suit or proceeding of a civil nature or any motion or hearing thereof, may be transferred, in the discretion of the court, from the division in which pending to any other division in the same district . Transfer of proceedings in rem brought by or on behalf of the United States may be transferred under this section without the consent of the United States where all other parties request transfer. (c) A district court may order any civil action to be tried at any place within the division in which it is pending. (d) Transfers from a district court of the United States to the District Court of Guam, the District Court for the Northern Mariana Islands, or the District Court of the Virgin Islands shall not be permitted under this section. As otherwise used in this section, the term “ district court ” includes the District Court of Guam, the District Court for the Northern Mariana Islands, and the District Court of the Virgin Islands, and the term “district” includes the territorial jurisdiction of each such court. (June 25, 1948, ch. 646, 62 Stat. 937 ; Pub. L. 87–845, § 9 , Oct. 18, 1962 , 76A Stat. 699 ; Pub. L. 104–317, title VI, § 610(a) , Oct. 19, 1996 , 110 Stat. 3860 ; Pub. L. 112–63, title II, § 204 , Dec. 7, 2011 , 125 Stat. 764 .) perturbation.justification: The modification from 'exclusive jurisdiction' to 'non-exclusive jurisdiction' directly contradicts the original agreement. While 28 U.S. Code § 1404 allows for a change of venue under certain conditions, the Law Explanation points out that it assumes an initial proper venue. The original contract specified an exclusive venue, and changing this undermines the initial agreement. The change implies that parties can litigate in any court, disregarding the original intent. This violates the agreement and the original intent of the parties. The legal snippets, like 28 USC 1404, permit a court to transfer a case. However, the Law Explanation highlights that this statute assumes an initial proper venue. An exclusive jurisdiction clause seeks to determine that initial venue.

perturbation.original_text: Each has had or will have employees to whom Confidential Information of the other is disclosed sign a Non-disclosure Agreement in content substantially similar to this Agreement and will notify the other in writing of the names of the persons who have had access to Confidential Information of the other party.
perturbation.changed_text: Each has had or will have employees to whom Confidential Information of the other is disclosed sign a Non-disclosure Agreement in content substantially similar to this Agreement and will notify the other verbally of the names of the persons who have had access to Confidential Information of the other party.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the notification method from 'in writing' to 'verbally' creates a conflict with standard business practices and potentially with statutes requiring written documentation for certain legal matters. It also makes it difficult to prove compliance.
perturbation.law_citation: Varies by State, e.g., California Civil Code § 1624
perturbation.law_explanation: While a notification clause is not directly governed by the Statute of Frauds, requiring it in writing strengthens the enforceability of the underlying confidentiality agreement by providing clear evidence of who had access to the confidential information. Removing the written requirement increases ambiguity and reduces legal certainty, therefore it could be seen as going against legal norms of documenting important business functions.

OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 2. MANNER OF CREATING CONTRACTS [1619 - 1633] ( Title 2 enacted 1872. ) 1624. (a) The following contracts are invalid, unless they, or some note or memorandum thereof, are in writing and subscribed by the party to be charged or by the party’s agent: (1) An agreement that by its terms is not to be performed within a year from the making thereof. (2) A special promise to answer for the debt, default, or miscarriage of another, except in the cases provided for in Section 2794. (3) An agreement for the leasing for a longer period than one year, or for the sale of real property, or of an interest therein; such an agreement, if made by an agent of the party sought to be charged, is invalid, unless the authority of the agent is in writing, subscribed by the party sought to be charged. (4) An agreement authorizing or employing an agent, broker, or any other person to purchase or sell real estate, or to lease real estate for a longer period than one year, or to procure, introduce, or find a purchaser or seller of real estate or a lessee or lessor of real estate where the lease is for a longer period
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: statute of frauds | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you statute of frauds Statute of frauds is a statute requiring certain contracts to be in writing and signed by the parties bound by the contract. The purpose is to prevent fraud and other injury. The most common types of contracts to which the statute applies are contracts that involve the sale or transfer of land, (see: real estate transactions ), and contracts that cannot be completed within one year. See also: Y amaha Motor Corp., U.S.A. v. Calhoun , 516 U.S. 199 (1996) Contracts for the sale of goods worth $500 or more. UCC § 2-201 [Last reviewed in June of 2024 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE commercial activities finance financial services housing PROPERTY landlord & tenant property & real estate law business law contracts wex definitions business sectors commercial transactions property law statutes Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The change from requiring written notification to verbal notification creates a contradiction. The Statute of Frauds, as described in both snippets, emphasizes the importance of written contracts and documentation to prevent fraud and ensure enforceability, especially for agreements with significant implications. While the notification clause itself might not be directly governed by the Statute of Frauds, the 'Law Explanation' correctly points out that requiring written notification strengthens the underlying confidentiality agreement by providing clear evidence. Changing this to verbal notification introduces ambiguity and makes it difficult to prove compliance, which undermines the purpose of the agreement and contradicts the legal norm of documenting important business functions.

perturbation.original_text: Any materials or documents that have been furnished by one party to the other will be promptly returned, accompanied by all copies of such documentation, after the business possibility has been rejected or concluded.
perturbation.changed_text: Any materials or documents that have been furnished by one party to the other will be returned within 180 days after the business possibility has been rejected or concluded.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'promptly returned' to 'returned within 180 days' introduces a significant delay that may be unreasonable and conflict with trade secret laws requiring timely protection of sensitive information.
perturbation.law_citation: Varies by State, e.g., California Civil Code § 3426
perturbation.law_explanation: The UTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. A 180-day delay in returning confidential materials could be construed as a failure to take reasonable steps to protect those secrets, especially if the materials contain highly sensitive or rapidly changing information, thus potentially undermining trade secret protection.

UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. perturbation.justification: The change from 'promptly returned' to 'returned within 180 days' introduces a delay that conflicts with the requirement for reasonable efforts to protect trade secrets. The Law Explanation states that a 180-day delay could be seen as a failure to take reasonable steps, especially with rapidly changing information. Scraped Law Snippet 1 mentions the Uniform Trade Secrets Act (UTSA), indicating the legal basis for trade secret protection, which the delay undermines, creating a contradiction. Although Scraped Law Snippet 2 is a 'Page Not Found' error, the mention of UTSA in Snippet 1 supports the potential for legal conflict.
